title: Camata
team: Anne-So Tisseyre, Valentin Giillet, Pablo Verdugo, Pedro Roca, Paola Ravagni, and Pamela Valenzuela
body_text:
Project Description

Camata is "a self-directed film operated by a learning machine, a manifestation of an inhuman entity enacted upon by a set of robotic operators, performing a final rite of passage on a human skeleton found unburied in the Atacama Desert." - Marian Goodman Gallery

"Camata’s live footage is the result of autonomous cameras decisions and is edited in real time. Sensors in the exhibition space capture a live human presence, disrupting its linearity." - MGG

My role in this project was to develop the back-end system responsible for real-time editing. I also worked on custom programming for on-site sensors that respond to human presence, enabling interaction between audience and system.

Special thanks to the PH Studio team for the camaraderie and guidance, Cadmos for the AV installation, Ivaylo Getov for support, and Tommy Martinez for connecting me to this project!

Touchdesigner

title: CRYPTO SIRENAS
body_text:
Projection Installation for The Goethe-Institut NY

Union Square, NYC

February, 2025

Project Description

'The Goethe-Institut New York is pleased to present the first New York institutional exhibition featuring a temporary collective with Berlin-based artists Anna Ehrenstein and Sunny Pfalzer, and San Salvador-based artists Lucy Tomasino and Alexa Evangelista, in collaboration with Web-3 researcher, Josh Davila.'

In December 2024, I began working with curator Zach Feldman to design a projector configuration for a retractable curved projection screen. Using floorplans of the space, and screen design from architect Chloe Munkenbeck, I designed a configuration that favored brightness, pixel density, and cost.

In February 2025, I led a team to install four ceiling-mounted projectors, and completed the projection mapping onto the 8.5m curved projection surface.

In addition to this, I programmed a custom back-of-house GUI in TouchDesigner to control content playback and system events. The content is being fed through MadMapper via NDI, and split to each projector with SDI-HDMI micro convertors.

Thank you to Zach Feldman for curating and producing the show, as well as Seth Scoville + Charlie Gangemi for projector installation assistance. Special thanks to Max Levin for connecting the dots, and Cam Vokey for pre-production support.

title: DIESEL SOUND OF THE BRAVE
body_text:
Creative Technologist, V2 for Chemistry Creative

Brooklyn, NYC

September, 2022

Project Description

The Diesel Sound of the Brave launch party was an event at Elsewhere in Brooklyn, intended for influencers and celebrities to celebrate the joy of fragrance. The Sound of the Brave!

I was involved in pre-production, pre-visualization, content creation, and installation for the event- part of the incredible team at Chemistry Creative, with stage design by Pete Sax, and V1 operations by Greg Zifcak

The main event was a stage design that included 2x LED walls and 8x large displays on either side of the stage. Throughout the night, DJs and performers would take the stage with a variety of Diesel fragrance content playing in the background.

There were also a number of smaller activations around the room, including a LED wall phot booth with a variety of backgrounds to choose from.

title: Elementa
body_text:
@ The Gallery

Project Description

"The Gallery, Downtown’s immersive new fine-dining spot, takes you on a visual and culinary journey through the elements." - TimeOut

Our team was asked to create a TouchDesigner system for an immersive dining experience. Using TouchDesigner and real-time hand and object tracking, we developed multiple interactive states for the various elements of the show.

Driven by the incredible creative and technical team at Mousetrappe Media, all of the creative direction and content was provided- we just had to make it work!

Using Dave's YOLO tracking module in TouchDesigner, Luke and I developed the interactive GLSL visuals for Aether, Air, Earth, Water, and Fire. Since our whole team was working remotely, we built each module in a way that could be easily integrated into the overall show control system.

Special thanks to the Mousetrappe team on the ground in LA who made this project happen! (Photograph: Michael Juliano for Time Out)

title: Fantastic Flora
body_text:
Project Description

This project was an immersive installation made for the very last edition of Shaker Mountain- a 'smart house and techno in a family-and-friends setting on 250 acres of tree-topped mountain land' [Resident Advisor]

I was involved in technical direction, integration, and installation of this project, working directly with designer + lead fabricator Shreya Patel.

This project began with a question "what would you want to see at a music festival?" Shreya imagined something that people could sit underneath and enjoy a moment of peace within a hectic festival atmosphere.

We began thinking of a large suspended flower, similar to a conch shell, where holding your ear up to it would allow you to hear the ocean- instead you would hear the calls and chirps of tropical birds from a rainforest far away.

Special thanks to Andrew Giugno / Lowres, and Nikki Cohen for throwing such a legendary party in the woods!

title: Incarceration Nations: A Global Docuseries
body_text:
Animator, Video Editor, VFX for Chemistry Creative

Global

2020 - 2022

Project Description

Incarceration Nations: A Global Docuseries is a mixed-media series about global mass incarceration- narrated entirely by those who have lived incarceration around the world, from England to El Salvador, Argentina to the USA, Brazil and Lebanon to South Africa and Sierra Leone.

The ten episodes of Incarceration Nations expose an international crisis while also focusing on solutions, showcasing the work of INN justice partners from one continent to another.

Ideal for classrooms, panel discussions, policymaker film screenings and more, Incarceration Nations is a powerful education about one of the greatest human—and civil—rights catastrophes of our time.

I was heavily involved in this project, working with Director Dr. Baz Dreisinger to create an educational series from the hours of interview footage an B-roll she has gathered over years of travels to prisons around the world.

This project would not have been possible without coordination by Sydney MacDonald, select illustrations by Matthew Wilson, assembly and editing by Nicolas Motyka- as well as the dozens of individuals who have shared their stories.

In 2021, the series was selected by the Tribeca Film festival, and has since been seen by thousands of people around the world in prisons, classrooms, museums, and community centers.

There is also an education guide available that includes graphics and information from the series.

Premeire Pro

After Effects

TouchDesigner

Ableton

Virtual Reality

Educational

Visual Effects

Concept Art

Sound Design

Project Highlights

'Virtual Reality'

Early on in the project, I developed an animation workflow using Virtual Reality painting using AnimVR.

This allowed us to explore abstract ideas through juxtaposition and dream-like sequences.

In combination with Interview and B-roll footage, I used these virtual reality animations to bridge the gaps of footage we had, while providing a unique visual identity to the entire series.

'Sound Design'

While Sound Design is not my primary focus as a creative, this project gave me an opportunity to explore the possibilities that sound provides.

In order to ground audience members in the unique geographic location of any given interview, I sampled public field recordings and ambient music from each country's region.

Sound mixing was ultimately completed by audio wizard and producer, Danny Taylor- which created a dream-like sense of time and place for each interview.

Traversable Dale · INN-EDUCATION-SOUNDSCAPE-JOHN

Traversable Dale · INN-EDUCATION-SOUNDSCAPE-IVAN

Traversable Dale · INN-EDUCATION-SOUNDSCAPE-ALMAR

Traversable Dale · INN-SOUNDSCAPE-SANTOS

Traversable Dale · INN-SOUNDSCAPE-AHMAD

'Visual Effects'

Similar to sound design, we knew that it would be important to create a sense of place for audience members as the series moves from country to country.

Using a combination of B-roll captured at the time of the interviews, aerial and satellite images, and found footage from the streets of any given city, I created a number of visual sequences in TouchDesigner to compliment the animation sequences.

Each sequence has a dissociative quality, with places blending between one another, as well as a depth map which resembles the view of our world through computer vision.

These sequences allude to the dissociative reality of returning to society after being locked away, as well as the dehumanization of individuals and society through the eyes of the system.

'Infographics'

Throughout the series there are a number of infographics and informational cards to inform the audience on some key facts about mass incarceration.

For these sequences I used a combination of After Effects, TouchDesigner, Procreate, and Photoshop.

Hand illustrations were created by Matthew Wilson and are used throughout the series.

'The Wave'

The heart of the installation is a series of interactive "time portals," each representing a different historical era. These portals are equipped with motion sensors, touchscreens, and augmented reality interfaces, allowing visitors to step into different time periods and explore them in an engaging and informative way.

The Portholes

Our team has meticulously choreographed the installation process to ensure that every element seamlessly fits into the overarching narrative. We've worked closely with artists, historians, and technologists to guarantee that every aspect, from lighting and sound to digital displays and physical artifacts, complements one another perfectly.

Communal Action Wall

Our team has meticulously choreographed the installation process to ensure that every element seamlessly fits into the overarching narrative. We've worked closely with artists, historians, and technologists to guarantee that every aspect, from lighting and sound to digital displays and physical artifacts, complements one another perfectly.

title: INTER_
body_text:
Creative Technologist for Chemistry Creative

Soho / Chinatown, NYC

2021 - 2023

Project Description

INTER_ is a 2-story interactive and immersive space located within the Historic National City Bank of New York, from creator Pete Sax, with the incredible team at Chemistry Creative.

My role as lead Creative Technologist included pre-production, development, and installation of the interactive tech and video components throughout the space.

I was also involved in creative development, and provided concept art and pre-visualization for key installations throughout the pre-production process.

The space contains dozens of projectors and 4k displays with hundreds of feet of video and data cabling, as well as a handful of large LED walls with unique pixel map configurations. Servers were configured by-hand during pre-production and installed on-site with a fiber network throughout the building.

As visitors move through the space,, there are a number of interactive moments that utilize live-camera feedback- unbeknownst to visitors at the time, these interactions are temporarily recorded and used to create generative art on display in the final room.

Integration with scenic, lighting, audio, production crew, and in-house staff was crucial throughout this project. Cheers to everyone involved!

Once guests are checked in for their experience, they are invited to wait in the orientation room.

This room acts as a holding chamber for timed groups, as well as an introductory film which guides visitors through a range of emotions and abstract thoughts.

There are 2x hidden NDI cameras in this room which capture preliminary interactions from guests.

Introductory film playing on a large LED wall at the start of the experience

LED mesh screen opposite main wall

After the conclusion of the introductory film, visitors head up a mirrored escalator.

On either side of the up-escalator, there is a built-in structure of angled mirrors and LED tubes. There are 3x ultrawide displays and an NDI camera hidden behind 2-way mirror panels. There is an additional NDI camera mounted on the ceiling at the far end of the 2nd floor, angled down the escalator.

The displays shine through the 2-way mirrors and appear within the mirror tunnel, while the camera feed is recorded and fed back into the system.

3x hidden displays and 2x NDI cameras were embedded into the structure

Video showing guests moving up the escalator past screens and hidden cameras

360 Projection pre-visualization for creative development- made with TouchDesigner

Once upstairs, guests enter a large ovular room with 360 degree audio and projections and LiDAR tracking.

I used TouchDesigner to create pre-visualization tools for creative development, as well as a unique visual sequence that was used as a part of the show.

Interactive 'Idle' state development- made with TouchDesigner

Custom visual sequence during a live show- made in TouchDesigner with Nicolas Motyka

Upon exiting the projection space, visitors move through a psychedelic garden full of lights and color. There are 6x Arduino units and ultrasonic sensors which control lights and sounds.

Wi-fi Arduino module and diagram of arch interaction

The 'Cymatic Well' (originally conceptualized by Taliesin Gilkes-Bower) is a large steel pan filled with water that can be controlled through saudio fequencies. I worked with a small team to prototype controllers and . After some iteration, we ended with 3 crystal balls that use a laser sensor- similar to the track ball of a computer mouse- and allow guests to control sound and play the pan like a large instrument.

There is a large transducer attached to the bottom of the well, which is controlled by an Ableton Live session. The vibration from this transducer creates a number of distinct resonant patterns in the water, which are visible on the surface. Due to the nature of cymatics and the shape of the vessel, only specific frequencies would create patterns on the water. Throughout development, I worked with Tommy Martinez to find the frequencies that would produce a pattern. Sound supervision was coordinated by Oliver Chapoy and MPC, with speaker installation by Danny Taylor.

There are also a number of unique visual interactions that are triggered based on specific user participation and are displayed on the large LED wall behind the well.

Guests can turn the crystal balls to control the lights and sounds-

Distinct patterns emerge in the water with certain frequencies.

Live demo with three users - 'ohm' state activated

Multi-color LED demo and visuals from pre-production

Once visitors are finished on the 2nd floor, they descend another escalator into a dark, rocky cavern.

After turning the corner and reaching the end of a long corridor, guests are invited to explore the walls of the chamber and look for motion activated control points. These control points work like a large organ, and play various ground-shaking tones when activated.

In the center of the room there is a volcano structure. Here, guests are invited to 'release' something into the fire. Inside the structure there is a hidden Leap motion controller, which allows users to affect the audio around the room, while their hand appears in the LED wall in front of them.

Atop the LED wall there is an Azure sensor which reveals a glowing magma character when activated.

Central chamber - showing LED mesh (center) and 'The Crucible' (right)

Process photos for leap motion placement- Crucible structure completed by Marta Kaminska

Interactive walkthrough- immersive rock corridor opens to main chamber with hidden hand sensors

Demo of Leap motion hand sensor and Crucible interaction

'Interconnected'

The final gallery replays the interactions of each user's experience, covered in all-white tiles and containing 10x large 4k displays. Each display is linked to a camera feed from a specific interaction, and sources recordings from the past 2 hours.

On either side of the room there is a large horizontal display containing all of the interactions that are playing in the room at that time. When a user steps in front of the frame, the image morphs into a live-camera feed, creating an infinite tunnel.

Overview of interaction recordings sourced throughout any given day

Live camera feedback - activated by motion on the 2x large, mirrored displays on either end of the gallery

Interactions recorded throughout the day are cycled through the generative system.

'The Wave'

The heart of the installation is a series of interactive "time portals," each representing a different historical era. These portals are equipped with motion sensors, touchscreens, and augmented reality interfaces, allowing visitors to step into different time periods and explore them in an engaging and informative way.

The Portholes

Our team has meticulously choreographed the installation process to ensure that every element seamlessly fits into the overarching narrative. We've worked closely with artists, historians, and technologists to guarantee that every aspect, from lighting and sound to digital displays and physical artifacts, complements one another perfectly.

Communal Action Wall

Our team has meticulously choreographed the installation process to ensure that every element seamlessly fits into the overarching narrative. We've worked closely with artists, historians, and technologists to guarantee that every aspect, from lighting and sound to digital displays and physical artifacts, complements one another perfectly.

Lobby + Misc

Upon entering and exiting the building, visitors can walk around a rectangular lobby area.

On the back wall there is a large TV mosaic with a unique arrangement of TVs. We worked to provide a cost-effective solution and provide preliminary content.

Guests can turn the crystal balls to control the lights and sounds.

Distinct patterns emerge in the water when activated with various pre-determined frequencies.

Sound waves can be visualized as they ripple through the water.

title: Ion Media Fan Connection Zone
team: Charlie Gangemi, Lake Heckaman, MPC, Pablo Gnecco
location: Vanderbilt Hall, Grand Central Station, NYC
body_text:
Associated Partners: Civic Entertainment, Ion Media

Project Description

This project was an immersive pop-up located inside the iconic Vanderbilt Hall in Grand Central Station, produced by Civic Entertainment and Chemistry Creative for Ion Media.

I was involved in content management, interactive development, and installation.

At the entrance to the Ion Fan Connection zone, there was a large video wall displaying clips from the television program FBI- mixed with live-feed surveillance cameras that were hidden around the activation. Guests could see themselves on the screen as they entered Vanderbilt Hall.

Located behind the FBI activation was an interactive goalie booth set up for the National Women's Soccer League, where guests could test their skills against a friend. Guests had the chance to pick their favorite NWSL team, which was selected through a Node Red browser operated by an Ion brand ambassador.

When a goal was made, a set of 2D LiDAR sensors would activate an animation sequence, triggering a huge celebration!

Special thanks to Lake Heckaman for LiDAR integration, and Pablo Gnecco + MPC for production coordination.

Touchdesigner

title: Kodansha House
team: Dave Tennent, Cam Vokey, Ashur Rayis
body_text:
Project Description

This project was an immersive pop-up located on Grand Street in SoHo, inspired by manga-media cult classics Akira + Attack on Titan.

Our team was asked to create 2 timeline-based video booths replicating moments from the Kodansha-produced anime classics.

I was responsible for replicating the compositions for both booths in TouchDesigner, as well as creating 2 functional GUIs for back-of-house monitoring and control. In addition to this, I was involved in the interactive development, installation, and monitoring of this project.

Special thanks to Susan Linns, Jeff Nash, Cam Vokey, Ashur Rayis, Dave Tennent, and many more who made this project happen!

Touchdesigner

title: LEDGER OP3N
body_text:
Creative Technologist, V2 for Chemistry Creative

Moonlight Studios, NYC

NFT.NYC 2022

Project Description

The Ledger Open was an event held at Moonlight Studios during NYC's NFT week 2022.

The amazing team at Chemistry Creative built and installed a number of unique display mounts and a variety of other scenic elements. I assisted in installation and was responsible for the content management, as well as the interactive hero moment at the front of the room.

When visitors approach the podium, there is an orange acrylic cube with a Ledger Nano suspended inside. Before the guest approaches, screens throughout the entire room are displaying an idle animation.

Once the guest touches the product (think Indiana Jones), the idle animation changes, and a new takeover animation floods the room. Working with Charlie Gangemi, we used TouchDesigner, a small pressure sensor, and hidden Arduino unit to achieve this effect.

Throughout the room there are a number of other displays with looping content provided by the client, as well as a large 'TV room' with a mosaic of displays suspended around and above the guest. I assisted in rigging and cabling this structure as well.

title: Miro Canvas '24
body_text:
Project Description

For this project, I worked with Cam Vokey to setup and monitor the product demo room for Miro's annual Canvas conference.

The demo room contained a custom wraparound desk which housed ten Mac Minis locked in kiosk mode. The staff wanted guests to be able to use the kiosks, log into their accounts, and access their Miro boards without accessing the desktop.

In addition to the kiosks, there were four in-house projectors mounted in the space. We used MadMappper to blend and color correct, and used QLab to drive playback.

I was involved with installation, troubleshooting, and on-site production.

Special thanks to Ben Baur and Livesight!

title: NIKE AIR SOHO
body_text:
Creative Technologist for Chemistry Creative

SoHo, NYC

February, 2020

Project Description

This project was the first large-scale multi-media TouchDesigenr installation of my career, thanks to the incredible team at Chemistry Creative

This installation was part of Nike's Air Max campaign, located on the ground floor of the Nike flagship store in SoHo.

I was involved in pre-production, content creation, interactive development, and installation of this project, working under Lovejoy Paradise. TouchDesigner legend Vincent Houzé also joined the project to focus on sensor placement and interaction.

When the box is idle, the Nike logo is displayed with a looping animation of clouds that I made in Blender. Once a guest steps inside the interactive zone, the clouds change color and a series of inspirational quotes appear on the screen.

Unfortunately, this installation took place in February of 2020, so it was only open to the public for a few weeks before the Covid-19 lockdown began. Such is life I suppose!

title: SPIEGEL IM SPIEGEL
body_text:
Project Description

Spiegel im Spiegel is a musical piece by Estonian composer Arvo Pärt. The name roughly translates to "Mirror within a mirror"

My involvement in this project began in 2022 through Zion Jackson and THE OFFICE performing arts + film, and has continued under Sophie Shakleton and Sound Postings.

My responsibilities have included researching, arranging, and experimenting with images- as well as timeline editing and final video delivery. The video itself is designed to accompany the live musical performance and could be modified for a variety of audiences around the world.

We began with images of our universe from the Hubble and James Webb telescopes, paying special attention to the visuals connections that emerge between the macro and micro world.

Soon after the first event in Paris, we began preparing an evolution of the sequence for another event in the Smokey Mountains of Tennessee. This sequence includes images from photographer Austin Mann, and aimed to connect deeply with the specific region of the performance.

The project continues to evolve with new sequences that speak to the geographic location of each performance, and has now been performed all around the world!

The Blue Paradox is an immersive exhibition at the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago, IL.

Much of what’s publicly available can be found on the museum’s page, which provides a window into the experience.

The exhibition was a major collaborative effort involving designers, programmers, and production crews, and it remains one of the most memorable installations I’ve had the chance to work on. It was a massive team effort, and one I’m proud to have been a part of.

Photos were taken on-site by Ben Bauer on his nice camera, or myself with my fuzzy camera.

title: THOUGHT PATTERNS
body_text:
Live Generative Visuals + Video Editor for Chemistry Creative

Brooklyn, NYC

2020 - 2021

Project Description

Thought Patterns is a live music performance and interview series created by Pete Sax- forged by the creativity and passion of a small, dedicated team including Nicolas Motyka, Sydney MacDonald, Christopher Robinson, Danny Taylor, Charlie Gangemi, Monty, Danny Ballester, Kevin Condor, myself, and all of the individual artists and bands involved.

Using the studio space at Chemistry Creative in Brooklyn, I collaborated with the team to orchestrate and capture over a dozen live performance videos, each boasting its own unique flair.

Working in TouchDesigner, After Effects, and Blender- I created generative, audio-reactive visuals for each performance, which would be projected or displayed on a large LED stage.

TouchDesigner was also used to control display surfaces and adjust visuals on the fly- which is endlessly rewarding in a live scenario!

I would also edit and publish videos weekly alongside Director Nicolas Motyka, and eventually managed the virtual production and sound design for the Thought Patterns short film 'Tether Tear'

Thought Patterns was born out of deep pandemic isolation in an effort to connect through music, and we were so incredibly lucky to have a space to work and explore our creativity.

Project Highlights

'The Wave'

The heart of the installation is a series of interactive "time portals," each representing a different historical era. These portals are equipped with motion sensors, touchscreens, and augmented reality interfaces, allowing visitors to step into different time periods and explore them in an engaging and informative way.

The Portholes

Our team has meticulously choreographed the installation process to ensure that every element seamlessly fits into the overarching narrative. We've worked closely with artists, historians, and technologists to guarantee that every aspect, from lighting and sound to digital displays and physical artifacts, complements one another perfectly.

title: XRP Ledger Apex 2025
body_text:
Project Description

"Hosted by Ripple, Apex 2025 is the official global community summit that unites developers, innovators, businesses, and investors for an exciting three-day celebration of blockchain technology and the incredible world of the XRP Ledger." - XRP Ledger Apex Website

Working alongside Dave Tennent and Trevor Grove, we created a multi-kiosk touchscreen experience for the XRP Ledger Apex conference in Singapore.

We translated Invisible North’s creative brief into a responsive system that invited users to explore a curated set of topics, express preferences, and receive a personalized persona.

The experience concluded with on-site pin that guests could wear with pride. We also facilitated a follow-up email featuring tailored content based on their preferences.

Built using TouchDesigner and deployed with real-time interactivity, the system blended playfulness with data collection to drive deeper audience engagement.

Special thanks to Dave and Sanaz, the entire CT team in Singapore, and Trevor Grove and the Invisible North team for all of the production work behind the scenes!
